+ REED SMITH LLP [4 Press Releases]

+ REED SMITH ADVISES NATURPAK ON SALE BY TRIVEST TO PPC

CHICAGO - Global law firm Reed Smith represented Trivest Partners and IPMF, LLC, doing business as NaturPak, a manufacturer of bone broths, soups, sauces, wet pet food, and food ingredients for national retailers and brands, on its sale by Trivest Growth Investment Fund II to Pritzker Private Capital ("PPC"). Trivest Growth Investment Fund II, managed by Trivest Partners LP, a private equity firm focused exclusively on investments in founder- and family owned businesses, partnered with NaturPak through a minority investment in 2020. During Trivest's partnership, NaturPak transformed from a small specialty co-manufacturer into a scaled, multi-segment platform, increasing its R&D and manufacturing capabilities and production capacity.

The cross-office Reed Smith deal team included attorneys in Chicago, New York, Denver, Philadelphia, Dallas, Orange County (California), Pittsburgh, Washington, Houston, Tysons (Virginia), and Miami. The team was led by Nick Gibson, Kristen Capriotti, Crockett Stevenson, and Sudeep Kalkunte, with support from Constantine Karides, Brad Schmarak, David Hayes, Simon Kliegman, Ian Sherwin, Paulo McKeeby, Daniel Hagedorn, Michelle Mantine, Cori Goldberg, A.J. Wissinger, Jillian Burstein, Tyler Thompson, Mike Summers, Michael Jacobs, Brendan Leanos, Benjamin Brimeyer, Michael Galibois, Michael Lowell, Joseph Mammone Jr., Michelle DiPuma, Batool Banker, Leah Hungerman, Taber Rueter, Clirae Bourke, Michaela Mapes, Peter Loy, Talia Boiangin Behar, Elliott Race, George Avdellas, Katherine Litaker, Taylor Kollmansberger, and Nikita Bazaj.

Source: Company Website

+ REED SMITH PARTNERS JOSH LOVE, ERIC MARDER, STEPHEN SESSA, AND EDWARD SHAPIRO NAMED TO VARIETY'S "DEALMAKERS 2025" REPORT

LOS ANGELES - Global law firm Reed Smith announced today that Josh Love, Eric Marder, Stephen Sessa, and Edward Shapiro, partners in the firm's global Entertainment & Media Industry Group, have been named to Variety's "Dealmakers 2025" report. Love, Marder, Sessa, and Shapiro are among an elite group of entertainment attorneys who have been chosen based on their more than $3 billion in music transactions in 2025, high‑profile client roster, and leadership across the music industry. The publication highlights their work on "sports and music transactions, start-ups and brands," "recording, music publishing, and live performance agreements (including Bad Bunny's upcoming Super Bowl halftime show)," and "catalog acquisitions."

This recognition reflects Reed Smith's continued leadership in high-stakes entertainment and music transactions, talent representation, and emerging legal issues at the intersection of entertainment, music, technology, and finance. The four partners were selected for their high-profile talent negotiations and representation business.

Source: Company Website

+ REED SMITH ADVISES LEON CAPITAL ON STRATEGIC INVESTMENT IN AVANTIS GROUP

LONDON - Global law firm Reed Smith has advised funds advised by Leon Capital on the strategic investment in Avantis Group, a UK-based provider of specialist engineering services to the maritime and energy industries. The transaction, which represents the latest new investment by Leon Capital, completed on 23 December 2025. The terms of the investment are confidential.

The transaction involved the Development Bank of Wales and Avantis Group's founders, Chris David and Tom David. Avantis Group's existing management team will retain operational control of the business. Blake Morgan acted as legal counsel to the Development Bank of Wales, while Acuity Law advised the founders. AMA Capital Partners acted as corporate finance adviser to Avantis Group.

Reed Smith advised Leon Capital on all aspects of the transaction, providing comprehensive M&A support in connection with the strategic investment. The matter involved a multi-disciplinary team spanning M&A, tax, and regulatory considerations.

The investment supports Avantis Group's long-term growth strategy across maritime and energy markets, including expansion into digital infrastructure and defence, and reflects Leon Capital's confidence in the company's differentiated engineering platform and experienced management team.

For Reed Smith, the transaction represents a further example of the firm's work advising private capital sponsors on complex M&A transactions and continues to strengthen its relationship with Leon Capital as it expands its platform.

The Reed Smith team advising on the matter was led by Andrew Houghton, with support from Ciaran Sweeney, Georgia Moorhouse and Selina Cook on M&A matters; Caspar Fox and Tom Baxter on tax; and Emma Weeden on FDI and antitrust.

Source: Company Website

+ REED SMITH STRENGTHENS ASIA-PACIFIC FINANCE AND TRANSPORTATION CAPABILITY WITH HIRE IN SINGAPORE

SINGAPORE - Global law firm Reed Smith announced the appointment of banking and finance partner Dr. Gregory Xu to its Singapore office. Xu, who joins from Stephenson Harwood, where he led the general banking and project finance offering in Southeast Asia and served on the firm's Supervisory Council, its executive committee, will join Reed Smith's Transportation Industry Group.

With over 15 years of experience, Xu advises on high-value, cross-border transactions across a wide range of sectors, including transportation - shipping, aviation, and logistics - trade, life sciences, energy, and technology. He regularly acts for financial institutions, funds, export credit agencies, leasing houses, asset owners, and Fortune 500 companies on complex financing, restructuring, and enforcement matters.

Xu has a strong track record in green and sustainability-linked lending and advises on multi-jurisdictional insolvency-related enforcement and restructuring matters. He also brings extensive experience in general corporate and commercial matters.

Richard Hakes, chair of Reed Smith's Transportation Industry Group, said: "Gregory is an outstanding addition to our team, bringing deep knowledge of ship finance, aviation finance, and banking throughout Southeast Asia. His arrival further strengthens our capabilities in Singapore and reinforces our commitment to the maritime, aviation, and broader transportation sectors across the Asia-Pacific region and beyond. Working closely with our shipping teams in Hong Kong and Shanghai and our aviation team in Singapore as part of our global Transportation Industry Group, Gregory will enhance our ability to provide seamless, cross-border support to clients operating in one of the world's most dynamic industries."

Xu commented: "Reed Smith's global platform, sector focus, and collaborative culture are hugely impressive. The firm's market-leading finance practice and its long-standing reputation in general banking, asset finance, and project finance were major draws for me. I'm delighted to be joining such a highly collegiate team with a clear strategy for growth in the region and look forward to contributing to the continued expansion of the firm's finance offering across Asia-Pacific."

Praj Samant, Reed Smith's Asia-Pacific managing partner, said: "We are delighted to welcome Gregory to the Singapore team. His arrival further strengthens our offering in one of the region's most strategically important sectors. We look forward to the valuable contributions he will bring to the firm locally and globally."

Xu is recognized as a leading practitioner by multiple legal directories. Chambers Asia-Pacific ranks him as a "Leading Individual" for Banking & Finance (India), while The Legal 500 identifies him as a "Leading Partner," "Next Generation Partner," and "Recommended" lawyer across Banking & Finance, Asset Finance, Projects, and Shipping in Singapore and in the foreign juris

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG, LLP [3 Press Releases]

+ GREENBERG TRAURIG ADDS ALEXANDRA CAMPBELL-DIEHL AS LABOR & EMPLOYMENT ASSOCIATE IN BOSTON

BOSTON - April 22, 2026 - Global law firm Greenberg Traurig, LLP has added Alexandra Campbell-Diehl to its Boston office as an associate in the firm's Labor & Employment Practice.

Campbell-Diehl advises employers on matters including single-plaintiff and class action employment litigation related to wrongful termination, harassment, discrimination, retaliation, and more, as well as workplace investigations and corporate transactions. Her work focuses on traditional labor law issues, including employer rights and obligations related to unions, and advising on union organizing, collective bargaining, and labor law developments. She has defended employers in attorney general investigations, state and federal litigation, and proceedings before the National Labor Relations Board, Department of Labor, Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, and the Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination.

Campbell-Diehl earned her J.D. from Boston College Law School and a B.S. from Cornell University.

"We are pleased to welcome Alex to the Boston Labor & Employment team," Terence P. McCourt and David J. Dykeman, co-managing shareholders of Greenberg Traurig's Boston office, said in a joint statement. "She brings a strong foundation in both employment law and traditional labor law, with experience advising employers on various employment matters in the workplace. Alex's addition enhances our capacity to support clients facing an increasingly dynamic and regulated employment landscape."

Source: Company Website

urn:contentItem:6JGF-SRG3-RTDN-M426-00000-00MILBANK LLP - INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENTS: LEADERS & PEERS2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisSTOCKMILBANK LLP - INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENTS: LEADERS & PEERS

DASHBOARD: MILBANK LLP

PermID4297973195Websitehttps://www.milbank.com/IndustryFull-Service BigLawAddress55 Hudson Yards, New York, NY 10001-2163

ACTIVITIES:

Milbank LLP, founded in 1866, is a global law firm headquartered in New York City. With over 800 lawyers across 12 offices, it serves clients in finance, energy, and infrastructure, specializing in project finance, M&A, and litigation. Generating over $1 billion annually, Milbank advised on major 2024 energy deals, per its recognition by Columbia Law School. Its culture fosters collaboration, diversity, and pro bono work, with 247 attorneys registered in New York. Competing with Sullivan & Cromwell, Milbank stands out for its finance expertise, with attorneys like John D. Franchini noted for excellence. In 2024, it expanded its renewable energy and AI practices. Recent initiatives include digital transaction platforms and ESG-focused financing, aligning with market demands.

+ MILBANK LLP [3 Press Releases]

+ 53 MILBANK LAWYERS FEATURED IN 2026 PRO BONO RECOGNITION LIST OF ENGLAND & WALES

Congratulations to the 53 Milbank lawyers featured in the 2026 Pro Bono Recognition List of England & Wales. The list recognizes barristers and solicitors who have given 25 hours of their time to pro bono work in the previous calendar year.

Source: Company Website

+ MILBANK ADVISES SOCIEDAD TRANSMISORA METROPOLITANA ON $630M GREEN RULE 144A PROJECT BOND OFFERING

Milbank LLP advised Sociedad Transmisora Metropolitana S.A. ("STM") in connection with the $630 million issuance of 6.385% green senior secured project bonds due 2055. STM is a leading Chilean power transmission company and a subsidiary of Grupo Saesa (a JV between Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan ("OTTP") and Alberta Investment Management Corporation ("AIMCo")). The proceeds of the bonds were used by STM, together with the proceeds of a bond issuance in the Chilean market, to repay a $1.1 billion syndicated credit facility used to finance Grupo Saesa's acquisition of Enel Transmision Chile, the principal power transmission company in Chile's metropolitan region.

Source: Company Website

+ MILBANK TRUSTS & ESTATES GROUP RANKED BAND 1 IN 2026 LEGAL 500 US PRIVATE CLIENT GUIDE

Milbank LLP's Trusts, Estates and Exempt Organizations Group is top-ranked for Private Wealth in New York in the 2026 Legal 500 US Private Client Guide. In addition to the firm recognition, co-practice group leader Austin Bramwell is featured as a "Leading Partner," and co-practice group leader Jessica Soojian is "Recommended."

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ DEBEVOISE & PLIMPTON LLP [3 Press Releases]

+ DEBEVOISE ADVISED ELLIOTT IN CONNECTION WITH ITS INVESTMENT IN STRATOLAUNCH

Debevoise & Plimpton LLP has advised Elliott Investment Management in connection with its investment in Stratolaunch, a hypersonics technology accelerator, to support the expansion of the company's hypersonic production and flight capabilities. For more information, please see the press release.

Source: Company Website

+ DEBEVOISE ADVISED KKR IN THE FORMATION OF ITS $2.5 BILLION ASIA PRIVATE CREDIT FUNDRAISE

Debevoise & Plimpton LLP has advised KKR in the formation of its $2.5 billion Asia private credit fundraise focused on committing capital to privately originated performing credit investments in Asia Pacific. For more information, please see the company's press release.

The Debevoise team was led by investment management partner Lorna Bowen and included partner Sheena Paul, counsel Christina Chen Marex and Huiyu Yin and associates Charlie Altman, Ali Curry, Chris Duff, Zach Ferguson, Ruth Fruchter, Emma Hauber, Patrick Zuliang Li, Lawson Miralha, Julia Glazer Needle, James Tai, Johanna Waber and Eike Bjorn Weidner, and tax partners Stephen Jordan and Daniel Priest and associates Shanna Adler and Jeannie Le.

Source: Company Website

+ DEBEVOISE & PLIMPTON LLP: MORE THAN $21 BILLION OF CFO TRANSACTIONS CLOSED IN PAST NINE MONTHS

Debevoise & Plimpton LLP has advised its clients across multiple Collateralized Fund Obligation (CFO) transactions with an aggregate value exceeding $21 billion over the past nine months. In addition to these CFO transactions, the Debevoise investment management and finance teams have advised clients in many single fund rated feeder transactions over the same time period.

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ SULLIVAN & CROMWELL [3 Press Releases]

+ SULLIVAN & CROMWELL: FDIC RESCINDS SUPERVISORY GUIDANCE ON MULTIPLE RE-PRESENTMENT NON-SUFFICIENT FUNDS FEES

Financial institutions commonly charge NSF fees when checks or Automated Clearinghouse ("ACH") transactions are presented for payment but cannot be covered by the balance in a customer's transaction account. After a check or ACH payment has been declined, it may later be resubmitted for payment (e.g., by a merchant), which may result in the customer's financial institution charging an additional NSF fee for the same transaction if the customer's account does not then reflect the necessary balance. The FDIC refers to these additional NSF fees as re-presentment NSF fees.

In its now-rescinded guidance, the FDIC had stated that failing to "clearly and conspicuously" disclose a practice of charging multiple re-presentment NSF fees could be both "deceptive" and "unfair" and therefore potentially a violation of Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices. The FDIC had also stated that consumer disclosures may not be sufficient to address potential unfairness to consumers, especially if "multiple NSF fees are assessed for the same transaction in a short period of time without sufficient notice or opportunity for customers to bring their account to a positive balance in order to avoid the assessment of additional NSF fees." The FDIC's guidance also described additional risks related to charging multiple re-presentment NSF fees, as well as risk mitigation practices and the FDIC's supervisory approach to addressing consumer harm. Source: Company Website

+ SULLIVAN & CROMWELL: GENIUS ACT IMPLEMENTATION

On April 7, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (the "FDIC") issued a notice of proposed rulemaking (the "FDIC NPR")[1] to implement provisions of the "Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins Act" or the "GENIUS Act.[2] The FDIC NPR proposes requirements that would apply to FDIC-supervised permitted payment stablecoin issuers ("PPSIs"), which in all cases would be subsidiaries of FDIC-supervised insured depository institutions ("IDIs"). The FDIC also proposes clarifications to its deposit insurance regulations, including that deposits held as reserves for a payment stablecoin would not be insured on a pass-through basis. The FDIC NPR also sets forth updates to the FDIC's regulations to provide that the technology or recordkeeping used by an IDI to record its deposit liabilities does not affect whether those liabilities constitute "deposits." This update would clarify that a tokenized deposit that meets the statutory definition of "deposit" under the Federal Deposit Insurance Act (the "FDI Act") is a deposit and is treated no differently under the FDI Act than other forms of deposits. Source: Company Website

+ SULLIVAN & CROMWELL: FEDERAL RESERVE PROPOSES EXPANSION THAT WOULD PERMIT USE OF FEDNOW FOR CROSS-BORDER PAYMENTS

On April 8, 2026, the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System issued a proposal to amend Subpart C of Regulation J, which governs the FedNow® Service. The proposal would permit FedNow participants to use intermediaries other than Reserve Banks to send funds transfers through FedNow.[1] This change would permit FedNow to be used for cross-border payments, aligning FedNow with the Fedwire® Funds Service, which is widely used for cross-border payments and has "permitted intermediaries for decades."

Currently, banks that are FedNow participants may not use intermediaries (other than Federal Reserve Banks) for funds transfers sent through FedNow.[2] This means that FedNow is not currently available for cross-border payments because both the originator and the beneficiary must be customers of U.S. banks. When the Federal Reserve announced details of FedNow in 2020, it acknowledged this limitation to domestic payments, but noted plans to consider expanding to cross-border payments in the future.[3]

In its current proposal, the Federal Reserve indicated that it expects that allowing participants to use intermediaries other than the Reserve Banks would enable cross-border payments to be processed through FedNow. In particular, either the sending U.S. bank or receiving U.S. bank would be permitted to act as a correspondent bank for a non-U.S. bank. As a result, an international portion of a payment could be processed through that correspondent bank outside of FedNow, with FedNow being used for the domestic portion of the payment.

Comments on the proposal will be due 60 days after publication in the Federal Register. Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ WILLKIE FARR & GALLAGHER LLP [3 Press Releases]

+ WILLKIE EMEA RESTRUCTURING PRACTICE EXCELS IN Q1 2026 LEAGUE TABLES

Willkie is recognized as a market leader in both the Octus and 9Fin restructuring league tables for the first quarter of 2026, earning multiple top-five rankings for its client work across an array of European restructuring matters.

Source: Company Website

+ WILLKIE ADVISES DELEK LOGISTICS PARTNERS ON NEW $1.3 BILLION CREDIT FACILITY

Willkie advised Delek Logistics Partners, LP, a public company specializing in the provision of midstream energy services, in connection with a $1.3 billion revolving credit facility with Truist Bank, the proceeds of which were used to refinance its existing credit facility with Fifth Third Bank, National Association, as well as for other general corporate purposes.

Source: Company Website

+ WILLKIE FARR & GALLAGHER LLP: HOW FAR DOES PRESIDENTIAL REMOVAL AUTHORITY GO?

On May 7 at 12:15 pm (ET), partner and Chair of the Appeals & Strategic Motions Practice Group Erica Ross will speak on the virtual panel titled "How Far Does Presidential Removal Authority Go?" hosted by the New York University School of Law. Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ LATHAM & WATKINS LLP [4 Press Releases]

+ LATHAM & WATKINS ADVISES LEADING SEMICONDUCTOR INFRASTRUCTURE INVESTOR MAVERICK SILICON ON FINANCING FOR MMTRON

mmTron, a semiconductor startup redefining the RF layer powering next‑generation connectivity, has announced US$45 million in Series A financing. Kenneth Safar at Maverick Silicon led the round with participation from Kelvin Khoo at Tipping Point Ventures, J&M, and other investors. This funding milestone endorses mmTron's demonstrated capabilities and its product‑market mission to redefine high‑performance RF connectivity.

Latham & Watkins LLP represented Maverick Silicon in the transaction with a corporate deal team led by partners John Miller and Haim Zaltzman, with associates Mason Taylor, Samantha McGarvey, and William Brown.

Source: Company Website

+ LATHAM ADVISES BLACKSTONE INFRASTRUCTURE IN INVESTMENT OF UP TO 2 BILLION IN EUROWIND ENERGY

Latham & Watkins has advised Blackstone Infrastructure on an agreement to invest up to EUR2 billion in Eurowind Energy, a leading pan-European renewables developer and independent power producer. The transaction is expected to close before the end of the year, subject to customary conditions.

Source: Company Website

+ LATHAM & WATKINS ADVISES CONCORD ON COMBINATION WITH BMG TO CREATE WORLDS LEADING INDEPENDENT MUSIC COMPANY

BMG and Concord have announced a definitive agreement to combine their businesses, creating the leading independent music company in the world. Operating under the BMG name, the combined company will bring together two highly complementary businesses to establish a scaled, global company delivering superior service to artists, songwriters, and partners - firmly grounded in an independent, artist-first model.

Latham & Watkins LLP advised Concord in the transaction with a corporate deal team led by partners Nadia Sager, Javier Stark, and Shanta Chirravuri.

Source: Company Website

+ LATHAM ADVISES ON ASIAN INFRASTRUCTURE INVESTMENT BANKS US$2 BILLION BOND OFFERING

Latham & Watkins has advised the underwriters on Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank's (AIIB) issuance of US$2 billion 3.875% notes due 2031. The firm previously advised on AIIB's US$1 billion offering in January 2026.

The Latham team was led by London corporate partner Ryan Benedict, with associates Valera Bamgbala, Caroline Koller Hill, and Tom Breheny.

Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ SKADDEN, ARPS, SLATE, MEAGHER & FLOM, L.L.P. [4 Press Releases]

+ SKADDEN, ARPS, SLATE, MEAGHER & FLOM, L.L.P.: LIFE SCIENCES ANTITRUST: RECENT EUROPEAN COMMISSION ENFORCEMENT AND TRENDS

The European Commission appears to be advancing a more expansive antitrust agenda in life sciences - one increasingly focused on innovation, exclusionary conduct and strategic interference with future competition. Counsel Michael Frese and European counsel Georgios Zacharodimos discuss how recent cases highlight the EC's efforts to prevent innovation suppression, unfair practices and anticompetitive conduct across the pharmaceutical sector, where merger reviews, abuse investigations and cartel enforcement are reshaping industry dynamics.

Source: Company Website

+ SKADDEN, ARPS, SLATE, MEAGHER & FLOM, L.L.P.: NINTH CIRCUIT ENJOINS CALIFORNIA CORPORATE CLIMATE RISK DISCLOSURE LAW, BUT THE WAITING GAME CONTINUES

On November 18, 2025, the Ninth Circuit stayed the enforcement of a California law requiring certain companies to publish a climate risk report. Whether companies will need to disclose their greenhouse gas emissions in 2026 remains an open question. Partners Raquel Fox and Virginia Milstead, counsel Caroline Kim and Liz Malone and associate Sydney Smith discuss the legal challenges, ongoing regulatory uncertainties and implications for companies' compliance planning.

Source: Company Website

+ SKADDEN, ARPS, SLATE, MEAGHER & FLOM, L.L.P.: NEW YEAR, NEW POLITICAL LAW TRENDS

Spending on federal lobbying reached an all-time high of approximately $4.5 billion in 2024 and continues to increase. Federal political action committees (PACs) raised $25.3 billion in the 2020 election cycle and $25.4 billion in the 2024 cycle, both more than doubling any previous election cycle, according to Federal Election Commission data. This upward trend continues in the 2026 cycle.

Source: Company Website

+ SKADDEN, ARPS, SLATE, MEAGHER & FLOM, L.L.P.: BANK OF ENGLAND REVISES ITS PROPOSED REGIME FOR REGULATING SYSTEMICSTABLECOIN

Bank of England proposals for regulating sterling-denominated stablecoins have shifted since 2023, but remain significantly more onerous than the FCA's proposals for non-systemic stablecoins in many respects. Partners Simon Toms and Sebastian Barling and associate Joseph Kamyar discuss the bank's latest proposals - including criteria for systemic importance, asset composition, capital and reserve standards, holding limits, legal claims and safeguarding measures - as well as the next steps for consultation and implementation.

Source: Company Website

+ SIMPSON THACHER & BARTLETT [3 Press Releases]

+ SIMPSON THACHER REPRESENTS INITIAL PURCHASERS IN $350 MILLION SUBORDINATED NOTES OFFERING BY TXNM

Simpson Thacher recently represented the initial purchasers, led by MUFG, Citigroup and RBC Capital Markets, in connection with an offering of $350 million in aggregate principal amount of 7.000% Fixed-to-Fixed Reset Rate Junior Subordinated Notes due 2056 by TXNM Energy, Inc. ("TXNM"). TXNM intends to use the net proceeds from the offering to repay short-term debt and for general corporate purposes.

TXNM is an energy holding company based in Albuquerque, New Mexico. Through its regulated utilities, Public Service Company of New Mexico and Texas-New Mexico Power Company, TXNM delivers energy to more than 800,000 homes and businesses across New Mexico and Texas.

The Simpson Thacher team for the transaction included Mark Brod, Barry Lau and Emily Anne Owen (Capital Markets); Jon Cantor and Jemma Li (Tax); and Jennie Getsin (FINRA and Blue Sky).

Source: Company Website

+ SIMPSON THACHER & BARTLETT MARK SKERRY REFERENCED IN MLEX ON CFIUS PRIORITIES AND OUTLOOK FOR 2026

Partner Mark Skerry was referenced in an MLex article examining the priorities and policy direction of the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States (CFIUS) last year and the trends to look for in 2026. Mark provided insights on emerging regulatory measures regarding foreign investment, noting that while CFIUS will continue to address national security threats, other federal and state-level measures shouldn't be overlooked. These measures, Mark observed, will have a significant impact on investment into and out of the United States in the coming year.

Source: Company Website

+ SIMPSON THACHER & BARTLETT GEOFF BAILHACHE AND JAMES HOWE QUOTED IN LAW.COM ON M&A OUTLOOK IN EUROPE

Geoff Bailhache and James R. Howe, co-heads of the Firm's European M&A practice, were quoted in Law.com on what to expect from dealmaking activity in 2026. James explained how "European deal activity strengthened notably through the second half of 2025, with large cap and mid-market transactions rebounding as assets that had been held back earlier in the year finally came to market." Geoff also highlighted "despite ongoing macroeconomic challenges, an improving IPO market in both the U.S. and Europe has provided an important tailwind, reinforcing confidence and helping to stimulate M&A activity."

Source: Company Website

value.Title: Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK9-6SS3-RTN1-V413-00000-00Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisNEWSWhy offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New YorkHerald Content Studio

New York's energy debate is often framed as a question of transition. But for many experts, environmental advocates, labor leaders and utility officials, the more immediate issue is one of necessity.

Electric demand is rising. Infrastructure is aging. Customers are grappling with utility costs. The need to combat climate change has long been discussed. And the state must determine how to add new power while reducing pollution and preparing for future growth.

The fastest way to add more power to the grid is through renewable energy, paired with battery storage. This will diversify the grid, provide stable sources of energy, and is the best option for the environment. That is why offshore wind, solar power and battery energy storage are central parts of the conversation.

A growing need for power

The New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) has predicted that energy demand is going to increase by 65% by 2040. This is straining our current grid and electric system. NYS is burning more fossil fuels to meet this demand, using aging infrastructure that should have been retired years ago. The product of these two major factors leads to increased reliability issues and negative environmental impacts.

Doug Schmid, an environmental science professor at Nassau Community College and executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island Group said:

"Our electricity needs are increasing. The New York State plan for our energy future is counting on 9,000 megawatts of offshore wind energy. We need it."

That demand challenge is one reason clean energy has become less theoretical and more practical.

While offshore wind comes with a lot of promise, it is part of a larger partnership that includes solar and storage that will complement each otherwhile providing reliability and predictability.

Health, air quality and climate

Investing in renewable energy doesn't just help meet the demand that is coming, it betters the environment that we rely on.

Adrienne Esposito, executive director of Citizens Campaign for the Environment, said renewable energy offers a direct contrast to fossil-fuel generation because it avoids combustion emissions.

"The wind blows, the turbines turn, and energy is produced," Esposito said. "The air quality is not impacted."

She also said communities are already experiencing the costs of climate change through rising seas, heavier rainfall and coastal damage.

"We have rising sea levels. We have more torrential rain events," Esposito said. "Long Islanders really are connected to our beaches, bays and our ocean. And yet climate change is changing all that."

Though Long Island is only one region, similar concerns are being felt across the state and the country.

Protecting the Long Island economy

Long Island's environment is not separate from its economy. Tourism tied to beaches, parks, boating, vineyards and waterfront communities supports jobs across Nassau and Suffolk.

Damage to coastlines and recurring flooding can carry direct costs, and Adrienne Esposito said climate impacts are already visible.

She highlighted how businesses are facing added costs, including shoreline infrastructure needing upgrades because of sea-level rise.

Insurance is another growing concern, particularly in vulnerable flood-prone areas where premiums can rise and coverage may become harder to secure.

"There is a direct correlation between climate change, living on the coast and our insurance rates for both our homes and our cars," Esposito highlighted. "Homeowners insurance here on Long Island, especially if you live south of Montauk Highway, is very difficult to get."

"Insurance companies have run the numbers, they've run the risk, they understand the dangers of climate change, and they don't want to insure homeowners anymore," she added.

This is being stymied by opponents

Renewable energy sources are not without their opponents. Brown University put out a report in 2023 mapping the network that has been built to stymie the industry.

They do this through funneling false narratives to trusted sources, creating doubt around the effectiveness or even environmental benefits of offshore wind.

This network claimed that marine life was negatively affected or that the infrastructure caused health issues for residents. This was an effort to make the projects too costly, whether in actual funding or political capital, to make them viable for the communities they serve.

"The misinformation campaign started with the fossil fuel industry," Doug Schmid, executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island group and Environmental Science professor at Nassau Community College. "You can go to the NOAA website. It says there's absolutely no evidence that offshore wind activity is causing any problems with marine mammals."

Instead, Schmid, who regularly performs post-mortem examinations on whales, said around 85% of the critically-endangered North Atlantic Right Whale are deaths related to gear entanglement from humans.

He also highlighted ways that offshore wind actually benefits the environment in some ways, creating hard spaces and rock beds for benthic animals to attach to.

"They're worried about the very little effects on birds or other disruptions, in comparison to an oil spill that kills hundreds of marine mammals and oil drilling that is 1,000 times greater intensity of sound," Schmid added.

These efforts were funded by fossil fuel interest groups and think tanks that were connected to the fossil fuel industry. This misinformation campaign continues today and has expanded to all renewable technologies because of the competition they are creating with energy generators that use fossil fuels.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

value.Title: Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK9-6SS3-RTN1-V2GB-00000-00Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisNEWSWhy offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New YorkHerald Content Studio

New York's energy debate is often framed as a question of transition. But for many experts, environmental advocates, labor leaders and utility officials, the more immediate issue is one of necessity.

Electric demand is rising. Infrastructure is aging. Customers are grappling with utility costs. The need to combat climate change has long been discussed. And the state must determine how to add new power while reducing pollution and preparing for future growth.

The fastest way to add more power to the grid is through renewable energy, paired with battery storage. This will diversify the grid, provide stable sources of energy, and is the best option for the environment. That is why offshore wind, solar power and battery energy storage are central parts of the conversation.

A growing need for power

The New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) has predicted that energy demand is going to increase by 65% by 2040. This is straining our current grid and electric system. NYS is burning more fossil fuels to meet this demand, using aging infrastructure that should have been retired years ago. The product of these two major factors leads to increased reliability issues and negative environmental impacts.

Doug Schmid, an environmental science professor at Nassau Community College and executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island Group said:

"Our electricity needs are increasing. The New York State plan for our energy future is counting on 9,000 megawatts of offshore wind energy. We need it."

That demand challenge is one reason clean energy has become less theoretical and more practical.

While offshore wind comes with a lot of promise, it is part of a larger partnership that includes solar and storage that will complement each otherwhile providing reliability and predictability.

Health, air quality and climate

Investing in renewable energy doesn't just help meet the demand that is coming, it betters the environment that we rely on.

Adrienne Esposito, executive director of Citizens Campaign for the Environment, said renewable energy offers a direct contrast to fossil-fuel generation because it avoids combustion emissions.

"The wind blows, the turbines turn, and energy is produced," Esposito said. "The air quality is not impacted."

She also said communities are already experiencing the costs of climate change through rising seas, heavier rainfall and coastal damage.

"We have rising sea levels. We have more torrential rain events," Esposito said. "Long Islanders really are connected to our beaches, bays and our ocean. And yet climate change is changing all that."

Though Long Island is only one region, similar concerns are being felt across the state and the country.

Protecting the Long Island economy

Long Island's environment is not separate from its economy. Tourism tied to beaches, parks, boating, vineyards and waterfront communities supports jobs across Nassau and Suffolk.

Damage to coastlines and recurring flooding can carry direct costs, and Adrienne Esposito said climate impacts are already visible.

She highlighted how businesses are facing added costs, including shoreline infrastructure needing upgrades because of sea-level rise.

Insurance is another growing concern, particularly in vulnerable flood-prone areas where premiums can rise and coverage may become harder to secure.

"There is a direct correlation between climate change, living on the coast and our insurance rates for both our homes and our cars," Esposito highlighted. "Homeowners insurance here on Long Island, especially if you live south of Montauk Highway, is very difficult to get."

"Insurance companies have run the numbers, they've run the risk, they understand the dangers of climate change, and they don't want to insure homeowners anymore," she added.

This is being stymied by opponents

Renewable energy sources are not without their opponents. Brown University put out a report in 2023 mapping the network that has been built to stymie the industry.

They do this through funneling false narratives to trusted sources, creating doubt around the effectiveness or even environmental benefits of offshore wind.

This network claimed that marine life was negatively affected or that the infrastructure caused health issues for residents. This was an effort to make the projects too costly, whether in actual funding or political capital, to make them viable for the communities they serve.

"The misinformation campaign started with the fossil fuel industry," Doug Schmid, executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island group and Environmental Science professor at Nassau Community College. "You can go to the NOAA website. It says there's absolutely no evidence that offshore wind activity is causing any problems with marine mammals."

Instead, Schmid, who regularly performs post-mortem examinations on whales, said around 85% of the critically-endangered North Atlantic Right Whale are deaths related to gear entanglement from humans.

He also highlighted ways that offshore wind actually benefits the environment in some ways, creating hard spaces and rock beds for benthic animals to attach to.

"They're worried about the very little effects on birds or other disruptions, in comparison to an oil spill that kills hundreds of marine mammals and oil drilling that is 1,000 times greater intensity of sound," Schmid added.

These efforts were funded by fossil fuel interest groups and think tanks that were connected to the fossil fuel industry. This misinformation campaign continues today and has expanded to all renewable technologies because of the competition they are creating with energy generators that use fossil fuels.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

value.Title: Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK9-2HM3-RTFT-751F-00000-00Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisNEWSWhy offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New YorkHerald Content Studio

New York's energy debate is often framed as a question of transition. But for many experts, environmental advocates, labor leaders and utility officials, the more immediate issue is one of necessity.

Electric demand is rising. Infrastructure is aging. Customers are grappling with utility costs. The need to combat climate change has long been discussed. And the state must determine how to add new power while reducing pollution and preparing for future growth.

The fastest way to add more power to the grid is through renewable energy, paired with battery storage. This will diversify the grid, provide stable sources of energy, and is the best option for the environment. That is why offshore wind, solar power and battery energy storage are central parts of the conversation.

A growing need for power

The New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) has predicted that energy demand is going to increase by 65% by 2040. This is straining our current grid and electric system. NYS is burning more fossil fuels to meet this demand, using aging infrastructure that should have been retired years ago. The product of these two major factors leads to increased reliability issues and negative environmental impacts.

Doug Schmid, an environmental science professor at Nassau Community College and executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island Group said:

"Our electricity needs are increasing. The New York State plan for our energy future is counting on 9,000 megawatts of offshore wind energy. We need it."

That demand challenge is one reason clean energy has become less theoretical and more practical.

While offshore wind comes with a lot of promise, it is part of a larger partnership that includes solar and storage that will complement each otherwhile providing reliability and predictability.

Health, air quality and climate

Investing in renewable energy doesn't just help meet the demand that is coming, it betters the environment that we rely on.

Adrienne Esposito, executive director of Citizens Campaign for the Environment, said renewable energy offers a direct contrast to fossil-fuel generation because it avoids combustion emissions.

"The wind blows, the turbines turn, and energy is produced," Esposito said. "The air quality is not impacted."

She also said communities are already experiencing the costs of climate change through rising seas, heavier rainfall and coastal damage.

"We have rising sea levels. We have more torrential rain events," Esposito said. "Long Islanders really are connected to our beaches, bays and our ocean. And yet climate change is changing all that."

Though Long Island is only one region, similar concerns are being felt across the state and the country.

Protecting the Long Island economy

Long Island's environment is not separate from its economy. Tourism tied to beaches, parks, boating, vineyards and waterfront communities supports jobs across Nassau and Suffolk.

Damage to coastlines and recurring flooding can carry direct costs, and Adrienne Esposito said climate impacts are already visible.

She highlighted how businesses are facing added costs, including shoreline infrastructure needing upgrades because of sea-level rise.

Insurance is another growing concern, particularly in vulnerable flood-prone areas where premiums can rise and coverage may become harder to secure.

"There is a direct correlation between climate change, living on the coast and our insurance rates for both our homes and our cars," Esposito highlighted. "Homeowners insurance here on Long Island, especially if you live south of Montauk Highway, is very difficult to get."

"Insurance companies have run the numbers, they've run the risk, they understand the dangers of climate change, and they don't want to insure homeowners anymore," she added.

This is being stymied by opponents

Renewable energy sources are not without their opponents. Brown University put out a report in 2023 mapping the network that has been built to stymie the industry.

They do this through funneling false narratives to trusted sources, creating doubt around the effectiveness or even environmental benefits of offshore wind.

This network claimed that marine life was negatively affected or that the infrastructure caused health issues for residents. This was an effort to make the projects too costly, whether in actual funding or political capital, to make them viable for the communities they serve.

"The misinformation campaign started with the fossil fuel industry," Doug Schmid, executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island group and Environmental Science professor at Nassau Community College. "You can go to the NOAA website. It says there's absolutely no evidence that offshore wind activity is causing any problems with marine mammals."

Instead, Schmid, who regularly performs post-mortem examinations on whales, said around 85% of the critically-endangered North Atlantic Right Whale are deaths related to gear entanglement from humans.

He also highlighted ways that offshore wind actually benefits the environment in some ways, creating hard spaces and rock beds for benthic animals to attach to.

"They're worried about the very little effects on birds or other disruptions, in comparison to an oil spill that kills hundreds of marine mammals and oil drilling that is 1,000 times greater intensity of sound," Schmid added.

These efforts were funded by fossil fuel interest groups and think tanks that were connected to the fossil fuel industry. This misinformation campaign continues today and has expanded to all renewable technologies because of the competition they are creating with energy generators that use fossil fuels.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

value.Title: Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK9-6SS3-RTN1-V46K-00000-00Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisNEWSWhy offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New YorkHerald Content Studio

New York's energy debate is often framed as a question of transition. But for many experts, environmental advocates, labor leaders and utility officials, the more immediate issue is one of necessity.

Electric demand is rising. Infrastructure is aging. Customers are grappling with utility costs. The need to combat climate change has long been discussed. And the state must determine how to add new power while reducing pollution and preparing for future growth.

The fastest way to add more power to the grid is through renewable energy, paired with battery storage. This will diversify the grid, provide stable sources of energy, and is the best option for the environment. That is why offshore wind, solar power and battery energy storage are central parts of the conversation.

A growing need for power

The New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) has predicted that energy demand is going to increase by 65% by 2040. This is straining our current grid and electric system. NYS is burning more fossil fuels to meet this demand, using aging infrastructure that should have been retired years ago. The product of these two major factors leads to increased reliability issues and negative environmental impacts.

Doug Schmid, an environmental science professor at Nassau Community College and executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island Group said:

"Our electricity needs are increasing. The New York State plan for our energy future is counting on 9,000 megawatts of offshore wind energy. We need it."

That demand challenge is one reason clean energy has become less theoretical and more practical.

While offshore wind comes with a lot of promise, it is part of a larger partnership that includes solar and storage that will complement each otherwhile providing reliability and predictability.

Health, air quality and climate

Investing in renewable energy doesn't just help meet the demand that is coming, it betters the environment that we rely on.

Adrienne Esposito, executive director of Citizens Campaign for the Environment, said renewable energy offers a direct contrast to fossil-fuel generation because it avoids combustion emissions.

"The wind blows, the turbines turn, and energy is produced," Esposito said. "The air quality is not impacted."

She also said communities are already experiencing the costs of climate change through rising seas, heavier rainfall and coastal damage.

"We have rising sea levels. We have more torrential rain events," Esposito said. "Long Islanders really are connected to our beaches, bays and our ocean. And yet climate change is changing all that."

Though Long Island is only one region, similar concerns are being felt across the state and the country.

Protecting the Long Island economy

Long Island's environment is not separate from its economy. Tourism tied to beaches, parks, boating, vineyards and waterfront communities supports jobs across Nassau and Suffolk.

Damage to coastlines and recurring flooding can carry direct costs, and Adrienne Esposito said climate impacts are already visible.

She highlighted how businesses are facing added costs, including shoreline infrastructure needing upgrades because of sea-level rise.

Insurance is another growing concern, particularly in vulnerable flood-prone areas where premiums can rise and coverage may become harder to secure.

"There is a direct correlation between climate change, living on the coast and our insurance rates for both our homes and our cars," Esposito highlighted. "Homeowners insurance here on Long Island, especially if you live south of Montauk Highway, is very difficult to get."

"Insurance companies have run the numbers, they've run the risk, they understand the dangers of climate change, and they don't want to insure homeowners anymore," she added.

This is being stymied by opponents

Renewable energy sources are not without their opponents. Brown University put out a report in 2023 mapping the network that has been built to stymie the industry.

They do this through funneling false narratives to trusted sources, creating doubt around the effectiveness or even environmental benefits of offshore wind.

This network claimed that marine life was negatively affected or that the infrastructure caused health issues for residents. This was an effort to make the projects too costly, whether in actual funding or political capital, to make them viable for the communities they serve.

"The misinformation campaign started with the fossil fuel industry," Doug Schmid, executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island group and Environmental Science professor at Nassau Community College. "You can go to the NOAA website. It says there's absolutely no evidence that offshore wind activity is causing any problems with marine mammals."

Instead, Schmid, who regularly performs post-mortem examinations on whales, said around 85% of the critically-endangered North Atlantic Right Whale are deaths related to gear entanglement from humans.

He also highlighted ways that offshore wind actually benefits the environment in some ways, creating hard spaces and rock beds for benthic animals to attach to.

"They're worried about the very little effects on birds or other disruptions, in comparison to an oil spill that kills hundreds of marine mammals and oil drilling that is 1,000 times greater intensity of sound," Schmid added.

These efforts were funded by fossil fuel interest groups and think tanks that were connected to the fossil fuel industry. This misinformation campaign continues today and has expanded to all renewable technologies because of the competition they are creating with energy generators that use fossil fuels.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

urn:contentItem:6JK9-6SS3-RTN1-V4N4-00000-00Why offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New York2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisNEWSWhy offshore wind, solar energy and battery energy storage are the future for New YorkHerald Content Studio

New York's energy debate is often framed as a question of transition. But for many experts, environmental advocates, labor leaders and utility officials, the more immediate issue is one of necessity.

Electric demand is rising. Infrastructure is aging. Customers are grappling with utility costs. The need to combat climate change has long been discussed. And the state must determine how to add new power while reducing pollution and preparing for future growth.

The fastest way to add more power to the grid is through renewable energy, paired with battery storage. This will diversify the grid, provide stable sources of energy, and is the best option for the environment. That is why offshore wind, solar power and battery energy storage are central parts of the conversation.

A growing need for power

The New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) has predicted that energy demand is going to increase by 65% by 2040. This is straining our current grid and electric system. NYS is burning more fossil fuels to meet this demand, using aging infrastructure that should have been retired years ago. The product of these two major factors leads to increased reliability issues and negative environmental impacts.

Doug Schmid, an environmental science professor at Nassau Community College and executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island Group said:

"Our electricity needs are increasing. The New York State plan for our energy future is counting on 9,000 megawatts of offshore wind energy. We need it."

That demand challenge is one reason clean energy has become less theoretical and more practical.

While offshore wind comes with a lot of promise, it is part of a larger partnership that includes solar and storage that will complement each otherwhile providing reliability and predictability.

Investing in renewable energy doesn't just help meet the demand that is coming, it betters the environment that we rely on.

Adrienne Esposito, executive director of Citizens Campaign for the Environment, said renewable energy offers a direct contrast to fossil-fuel generation because it avoids combustion emissions.

"The wind blows, the turbines turn, and energy is produced," Esposito said. "The air quality is not impacted."

She also said communities are already experiencing the costs of climate change through rising seas, heavier rainfall and coastal damage.

"We have rising sea levels. We have more torrential rain events," Esposito said. "Long Islanders really are connected to our beaches, bays and our ocean. And yet climate change is changing all that."

Though Long Island is only one region, similar concerns are being felt across the state and the country.

Protecting the Long Island economy

Long Island's environment is not separate from its economy. Tourism tied to beaches, parks, boating, vineyards and waterfront communities supports jobs across Nassau and Suffolk.

Damage to coastlines and recurring flooding can carry direct costs, and Adrienne Esposito said climate impacts are already visible.

She highlighted how businesses are facing added costs, including shoreline infrastructure needing upgrades because of sea-level rise.

Insurance is another growing concern, particularly in vulnerable flood-prone areas where premiums can rise and coverage may become harder to secure.

"There is a direct correlation between climate change, living on the coast and our insurance rates for both our homes and our cars," Esposito highlighted. "Homeowners insurance here on Long Island, especially if you live south of Montauk Highway, is very difficult to get."

"Insurance companies have run the numbers, they've run the risk, they understand the dangers of climate change, and they don't want to insure homeowners anymore," she added.

This is being stymied by opponents

Renewable energy sources are not without their opponents. Brown University put out a report in 2023 mapping the network that has been built to stymie the industry.

They do this through funneling false narratives to trusted sources, creating doubt around the effectiveness or even environmental benefits of offshore wind.

This network claimed that marine life was negatively affected or that the infrastructure caused health issues for residents. This was an effort to make the projects too costly, whether in actual funding or political capital, to make them viable for the communities they serve.

"The misinformation campaign started with the fossil fuel industry," Doug Schmid, executive committee member of the Sierra Club Long Island group and Environmental Science professor at Nassau Community College. Instead, Schmid, who regularly performs post-mortem examinations on whales, said around 85% of the critically-endangered North Atlantic Right Whale are deaths related to gear entanglement from humans.

He also highlighted ways that offshore wind actually benefits the environment in some ways, creating hard spaces and rock beds for benthic animals to attach to.

"They're worried about the very little effects on birds or other disruptions, in comparison to an oil spill that kills hundreds of marine mammals and oil drilling that is 1,000 times greater intensity of sound," Schmid added.

These efforts were funded by fossil fuel interest groups and think tanks that were connected to the fossil fuel industry. This misinformation campaign continues today and has expanded to all renewable technologies because of the competition they are creating with energy generators that use fossil fuels.

Until recently, the energy market was dominated by fossil fuels in America. A transition to clean energy generation has threatened that reliance and the transition has been proven to be a viable path.

The road ahead

No single technology is expected to power New York's future alone.

But offshore wind can provide large-scale generation. Solar can reduce daytime demand. Battery storage can help stabilize the system and optimize the electric grid when energy is needed most.

Together, experts say, these technologies represent a practical path toward meeting growth, improving affordability and creating jobs while cutting emissions.

As demand rises, the question may no longer be whether New York needs these resources, but how quickly it can deploy them.

urn:contentItem:6JJX-K1T3-RVC5-M0HV-00000-00Hubbell Incorporated (NYSE:HUBB) Expands Electrical Solutions Portfolio With $3 Billion NSI Acquisition2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisHubbell Incorporated (NYSE:HUBB) Expands Electrical Solutions Portfolio With $3 Billion NSI Acquisition

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

12 May 2026 15:15 EDT

On 9th May 2026, Hubbell Incorporated (NYSE:HUBB) agreed to acquire NSI Industries, a Huntersville, North Carolina-based manufacturer, for $3 billion. The deal adds more than 15,000 branded electrical products to Hubbell's portfolio, including Bridgeport fittings, Polaris connectors, and Tork timers. NSI is projected to generate approximately $570 million in revenue in 2026.

The transaction is expected to close in mid-2026, subject to customary regulatory approvals. Gerben Bakker, CEO, president and chairman of Hubbell, says NSI complements Hubbell's Electrical Solutions portfolio, and electrification trends are expected to drive growth across light industrial, data center and network infrastructure applications. Hubbell is acquiring NSI from Sentinel Capital Partners, a New York City-based private equity firm

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 HUBBELL RECENT PRESS RELEASES

SECTION 2 HUBBELL ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 HUBBELL RECENT PRESS RELEASES

February 03: Hubbell quarterly dividends steady

Hubbell today announced a quarterly dividend of $1.42 per share for Q4/2025; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q3/2025) dividend of $1.42. The ex-dividend date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and the record date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and it is payable on Monday, March 16.

February 03: Hubbell quarterly dividends steady

Hubbell today announced a quarterly dividend of $1.42 per share for Q4/2025; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q3/2025) dividend of $1.42. The ex-dividend date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and the record date is Friday, February 27, 2026 and it is payable on Monday, March 16.

October 28, 2025: Hubbell quarterly dividends up 7.6% sequentially from previous quarter

Hubbell today announced a quarterly dividend of $1.42 per share for Q3/2025; up 7.6% sequentially from the previous quarter (Q2/2025) dividend of $1.32. The ex-dividend date is Friday, November 28, 2025 and the record date is Friday, November 28, 2025 and it is payable on Monday, December 15.

SECTION 2 HUBBELL ACTIVITIES

Activities

Hubbell Incorporated, listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE: HUBB), is a leading manufacturer and marketer of electrical and utility solutions, serving diverse sectors including utilities, commercial, and industrial markets. The company's core business activities involve designing, producing, and distributing a wide range of products such as electrical distribution equipment, power transmission components, lighting fixtures, and connectors, which support critical infrastructure needs. Among its fastest-growing segments are utility solutions and electrical products focused on renewable energy integration and smart grid technologies, driven by rising global demand for grid modernization, energy efficiency, and sustainable power systems. This expansion is bolstered by investments in data centers and electrification trends, reinforcing Hubbell's role as a key innovator in the electrical industry with a strong emphasis on reliability and technological advancement. It is the NYSE's 7th largest Industrial company by market capitalisation.

EVERUS CONSTRUCTION GROUP ACTIVITIES

Everus Construction Group, Inc., a company listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading provider of comprehensive construction and engineering services. Its core activities include general contracting, project management, and design-build solutions across sectors such as infrastructure, energy, and commercial real estate, with a strong emphasis on delivering high-quality, cost-effective projects. The company has seen robust growth in its renewable energy and sustainable infrastructure segments, fueled by rising demand for green initiatives and government investments in clean technology. Additionally, its data center and digital infrastructure divisions are expanding rapidly, capitalizing on the surge in technology adoption and e-commerce. Everus maintains a commitment to innovation, safety, and efficiency, positioning it as a key player in modernizing global infrastructure while adapting to evolving regulatory and environmental standards. It is the NYSE's 13th largest Construction company by market capitalisation.

SECTION 1 RECENT NEWS

Recent News 1 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 327 words

Wed Apr 29, 2026: Corning's Springboard Plan Extended Amid Gen AI and Solar Growth

Corning Incorporated (NYSE:GLW) has announced a robust start to 2026, reporting a 18% increase in Q1 sales year over year, buoyed primarily by its Optical Communications and Solar segments. The company's sales reached approximately US$4.14 billion, reflecting substantial demand from hyperscale customers, including Meta, for Gen AI infrastructure development. The date of this announcement is the 29th of April, 2026.

The recent performance underscores Corning's strategic positioning at the intersection of two significant growth trends: the surge in demand for data center capabilities driven by Gen AI and the expanding market for renewable energy solutions. In a bid to capitalize on these trends, Corning has extended and upgraded its Springboard growth initiative through 2030, aligning its operational focus with long-term commitments made by its large customers.

Corning's extended Springboard plan indicates a commitment to developing its optical and materials portfolio in response to anticipated demand in the Gen AI and solar sectors. The company has recently secured multiple long-term contracts with hyperscale clients, which not only improve revenue visibility but also signal a shift towards more stable, multi-year project planning. This approach mitigates the risk associated with short-term fluctuations in order volumes.

Financial results from Q1 2026 reveal significant progress, with net income rising to US$371 million compared to US$157 million in the same quarter last year. Earnings per diluted share from continuing operations stood at US$0.43, up from US$0.18. Despite these promising figures, investors should remain cautious about the risks associated with customer concentration and reliance on specific market segments, as delays or downturns in Gen AI or solar demand could impact future performance.

Moving forward, investors are encouraged to monitor how quickly Corning's hyperscale agreements translate into tangible orders and whether its guidance for Q2 2026 sales of approximately US$4.6 billion holds steady. Moreover, the company's funding strategy for Springboard-related capacity expansions and competitive dynamics within the optical networking and solar materials markets will be critical in determining Corning's trajectory in these rapidly evolving sectors.

Recent News 2 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 327 words

Tue Apr 21, 2026: Corning's Strategic Focus on Advanced Optics: A Shift in Semiconductor Dynamics?

Corning Incorporated's recent advancements in optics may subtly reshape its narrative within the semiconductor sector. On the 20th of April, 2026, Corning's Commercial Technology Director for Advanced Optics, Xavier Lafosse, took the stage at the International Semiconductor Industry Group (ISIG) Symposium in Sunnyvale, California, to showcase the company's high-performance optics designed for the semiconductor ecosystem.

The ISIG forum provided a targeted platform for Corning to discuss how its advanced optics can integrate into future semiconductor manufacturing and data infrastructure. This presentation is pivotal as it aligns with Corning's ongoing initiatives, including the Springboard plan and recent announcements surrounding AI-focused optical products and a significant fiber agreement with Meta. These developments suggest that Corning is strategically positioning itself to capitalize on growth opportunities within the optical communications space.

Investors looking at Corning's future must consider the company's ambitious projections, which include anticipated revenues of $24.3 billion and earnings of $3.4 billion by 2029. These figures imply a necessary annual revenue growth rate of 15.8% and an increase in earnings of approximately $1.8 billion from current levels. However, achieving these targets involves navigating potential risks, including dependence on external market trends and the impact of tariffs or macroeconomic fluctuations.

Despite these challenges, there remains a degree of optimism among analysts. Some predict that Corning could achieve revenues nearing $22.2 billion and earnings around $3.5 billion by 2028, fueled by a substantial payoff from its AI optics segment. Nonetheless, concerns regarding customer concentration and the pace of innovation monetization underscore the need for investors to carefully evaluate the balance of risks and potential rewards following Corning's recent optics push.

As Corning continues to refine its positioning within the semiconductor landscape, stakeholders are encouraged to conduct thorough research. This includes considering divergent viewpoints on the company's future performance and assessing the fundamental health of Corning through comprehensive analyses and visual tools. With the dynamics of the semiconductor market evolving, how Corning navigates this landscape will be crucial for its long-term success.

Recent News 3 of 3, Source: HourlyBusinessNews, 328 words

Thu Apr 16, 2026: Corning Partners with Meta to Enhance AI Infrastructure

Corning Incorporated has solidified its position as a significant player in the AI infrastructure market through a new fiber optic partnership with Meta Platforms. This strategic collaboration aims to enhance high-bandwidth connectivity essential for artificial intelligence applications. On the 16th of April, 2026, Corning also announced its commitment to investing in U.S. solar wafer manufacturing, thereby reinforcing its role in the domestic solar supply chain.

These initiatives strategically connect Corning directly to two critical sectors: hyperscale data networks that facilitate AI growth and the ongoing expansion of solar energy capabilities in the United States. As the demand for AI infrastructure surges, Corning is positioning itself at the intersection of technological advancement and sustainable energy solutions, which are increasingly vital in today's market landscape.

For investors monitoring Corning, the company's recent performance is noteworthy. Shares recently traded at $168.27, reflecting an impressive return of 27.4% over the past 30 days and an astonishing 85.6% year-to-date. Over the past year, Corning's stock has soared by 313.8%, with robust growth also evident over the last three and five-year periods, indicating a significant upward trajectory in its valuation.

The partnership with Meta places Corning at the forefront of AI infrastructure, while its involvement in solar wafer production aligns with broader energy and industrial policy trends in the U.S. For long-term investors, these developments highlight Corning's unique position amidst the dual narratives of AI data expansion and the transition to localized clean energy supply chains. However, potential investors should remain cautious, as Corning's shares are currently trading approximately 25% above the analyst price target of $134.93 and are deemed overvalued by some analysts.

Additionally, Corning faces challenges, including a high level of debt, which could pose risks should market conditions change or if capital projects are delayed. With a current P/E ratio of about 90.6 compared to the industry average of 31.9, monitoring its valuation in relation to peers will be crucial as the company moves forward with its AI and solar initiatives.

value.Title: Critical Metals Corporation to Present at the Precious Metals & Critical Minerals Virtual Investor Conference May 5th Company invites individual and institutional investors, as well as advisors and analysts, to attend online at VirtualInvestorConferences.com

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-YKF3-S6G7-W359-00000-00Critical Metals Corporation to Present at the Precious Metals & Critical Minerals Virtual Investor Conference May 5th2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:02ZLexisNexisCALENDAR OF EVENTSCritical Metals Corporation to Present at the Precious Metals & Critical Minerals Virtual Investor Conference May 5thCompany invites individual and institutional investors, as well as advisors and analysts, to attend online at VirtualInvestorConferences.com

NEW YORK, May 04, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Critical Metals Corporation (NASDAQ:CRML), based in British Virgin Islands, focused on Rare Earths & Critical Materials, today announced that Tom McNamara, Director of Corporate Development, will present live at the Precious Metals & Critical Minerals Virtual Investor Conference hosted by VirtualInvestorConferences.com, on May 5th, 2026

DATE: May 5thTIME: 2:00 PM

REGISTER HERE

Available for 1x1 meetings: April 5th. Schedule 1x1 Meetings here.

This will be a live, interactive online event where investors are invited to ask the company questions in real-time. If attendees are not able to join the event live on the day of the conference, an archived webcast will also be made available after the event.

It is recommended that online investors pre-register and run the online system check to expedite participation and receive event updates.

Learn more about the event at www.virtualinvestorconferences.com.

Recent Company Highlights

Pilot Plant confirms proof of concept technically for Tanbreez

Romanian JV is a full Mine-to-Magnet supply-chain for NATO

Announced offer to acquire European Lithium

Secured 92.5% ownership of Tanbreez at the asset level.

Announced detail spending/activity plans for 2026

About Critical Metals Corp. 

Critical Metals Corp. is a forward-looking resource company focused on unlocking the potential of rare earth elements critical to defense applications, advanced technologies, and the global clean energy transition.

With a strategic presence in Greenland, we are developing one of the world’s most promising rare earth deposits to help meet the rising global demand for secure, sustainable, and responsible sources of critical minerals.

Our mission is to facilitate a fully integrated supply chain—from exploration and extraction to advanced materials production—that aligns with the highest environmental standards and delivers long-term value to stakeholders.

As the world shifts toward electrification, renewable energy, and high-tech innovation, Critical Metals Corp. is proud to play a key role in delivering the essential materials that power the future.

About Virtual Investor Conferences®Virtual Investor Conferences (VIC) is the leading proprietary investor conference series that provides an interactive forum for publicly traded companies to seamlessly present directly to investors.

Providing a real-time investor engagement solution, VIC is specifically designed to offer companies more efficient investor access.  Replicating the components of an on-site investor conference, VIC offers companies enhanced capabilities to connect with investors, schedule targeted one-on-one meetings and enhance their presentations with dynamic video content. Accelerating the next level of investor engagement, Virtual Investor Conferences delivers leading investor communications to a global network of retail and institutional investors.

CONTACTS:Critical Metals CorporationName Tom McNamaraTitle     Director of Corporate DevelopmentPhone 203-554-4009Email  thomasm@criticalmetalscorp.com

Virtual Investor Conferences Greg YoungVP Corporate Services OTC Markets Group (212) 652-5958 greg@otcmarkets.com
